wrong_choice_explanations: ます is the polite present/future form — it means 'will watch' but this sentence is about yesterday.
ません is polite negative — it means 'don't watch' but this sentence is about completing an action.
ませんでした is polite past negative — it means 'didn't watch' but this sentence is positive.
ます is the polite present/future form — it means 'will eat' but this sentence is about completed action.
た is the plain past form — it's too casual for this polite sentence structure.
ませんでした is polite past negative — it means 'didn't eat' but this sentence is positive.
ます is the polite present/future form — it means 'will talk' but this sentence is about past action.
ません is polite negative — it means 'don't talk' but this sentence describes completed action.
た is the plain past form — it's too casual for this polite sentence structure.
ます is the polite present/future form — it means 'will do' but this sentence is about completed homework.
ませんでした is polite past negative — it means 'didn't do' but this sentence is about completing homework.
ています indicates ongoing action — it means 'am doing' but this sentence describes completed action.

exercises.translation: I read a book at the library.
wrong_choice_explanations: ます is the polite present/future form — it means 'will read' but this sentence is about past action.
ません is polite negative — it means 'don't read' but this sentence describes completed reading.
たい indicates desire — it means 'want to read' but this sentence describes completed action.
ます is the polite present/future form — it means 'will buy' but this sentence is about completed purchase.
ませんでした is polite past negative — it means 'didn't buy' but this sentence is positive.
ていました indicates past ongoing action — it suggests prolonged buying activity rather than a completed purchase.
ます is the polite present/future form — it means 'will listen' but this sentence is about past action.
ません is polite negative — it means 'don't listen' but this sentence describes completed listening.
なかった is plain past negative — it's too casual for this polite sentence and also negative.

exercises.translation: I took a walk in the park.
wrong_choice_explanations: ます is the polite present/future form — it means 'will walk' but this sentence is about past action.
ませんでした is polite past negative — it means 'didn't walk' but this sentence is positive.
よう is volitional form — it means 'let's walk' but this sentence describes completed action.
ます is the polite present/future form — it means 'will call' but this sentence is about completed call.
ません is polite negative — it means 'don't call' but this sentence describes completed action.
る is dictionary form ending — it can't attach to the masu-stem かけ in this context.
ます is the polite present/future form — it means 'will study' but this sentence is about past action.
ませんでした is polite past negative — it means 'didn't study' but this sentence is positive.
ていた is plain past progressive — it's too casual for this polite sentence structure.
